title: Russian October Revolution
summary: On the night of 7 November 1917, 25 October by the old Julian calendar still in Russian use, armed workers, soldiers, and sailors organised by the Bolshevik Party seized the key installations of the capital Petrograd and, before dawn on the 8th, arrested the Provisional Government in the Winter Palace. Vladimir Lenin proclaimed the transfer of power to the soviets; within months the Bolsheviks had made a separate peace with Germany, dissolved the Constituent Assembly, and begun to build the world's first avowedly socialist state. The Russian Revolution reshaped the twentieth century.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: Provisional Government troops firing on demonstrators in Petrograd during the July Days of 1917.

sections.body:
The Russian Empire entered the twentieth century as a vast, largely peasant autocracy under the Romanov dynasty. The 1905 revolution, sparked by defeat in the Russo-Japanese War and the massacre of unarmed petitioners on Bloody Sunday, had forced Tsar Nicholas II to concede an elected Duma and civil liberties, but he chased its powers back throughout the following decade. Industrial workers concentrated in Petrograd, Moscow, and the Donbas were radicalised by long hours, low wages, and the memory of tsarist violence.

The First World War completed what economic strain had begun. Russia mobilised some fifteen million men over four years. By 1917 defeats in Poland and Galicia had cost roughly two million dead; food and fuel shortages in the cities were acute; the tsar's decision to take personal command at the front in 1915 left the government in the hands of his German-born wife Alexandra and the mystic Grigori Rasputin, whose murder by aristocrats in December 1916 came too late. Bread queues and strikes in the capital, renamed Petrograd from the German-sounding Sankt-Peterburg, broke into open protest on International Women's Day, 8 March 1917 by the Western calendar. Within a week the Petrograd garrison had refused to fire on the crowds, the Duma had formed a Provisional Committee, and on 15 March Nicholas II abdicated in a railway carriage at Pskov, ending three centuries of Romanov rule.

What followed was a period of dual power. A Provisional Government drawn from liberal and moderate socialist Duma deputies shared authority with the Petrograd Soviet of Workers' and Soldiers' Deputies, which controlled the loyalty of the troops and the postal and railway workers. The government kept Russia in the war and postponed both land redistribution and the summoning of a Constituent Assembly. In April 1917 the German high command allowed Vladimir Ilich Ulyanov, known as Lenin, to travel from Swiss exile through Germany in a sealed train, calculating rightly that his return to Petrograd's Finland Station would deepen chaos. Lenin's April Theses demanded 'all power to the soviets' and immediate peace. A failed offensive in June, an attempted July uprising, and a botched military coup by General Lavr Kornilov in August progressively discredited the government led from July by the socialist lawyer Alexander Kerensky and armed the Bolshevik-led Red Guards.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: The cruiser Aurora, whose blank round on the evening of 7 November 1917 signalled the assault on the Winter Palace.

sections.body:
By the autumn of 1917 the Bolsheviks held majorities in the Petrograd and Moscow soviets. On 23 October (old style 10 October) the Bolshevik Central Committee, meeting in a Petrograd apartment, voted by ten to two, over the objections of Grigory Zinoviev and Lev Kamenev, to prepare an armed insurrection. The Petrograd Soviet's Military Revolutionary Committee, chaired by Lev Trotsky, took operational command of the loyal troops and Red Guards. The plan was to time the seizure of power to the opening of the Second All-Russian Congress of Soviets on 7 November so that the takeover could be presented as a transfer of authority to the soviets rather than a party coup.

On the night of 6-7 November (24-25 October old style) Kerensky's attempts to shut Bolshevik newspapers and telephone lines gave the pretext. Detachments of Red Guards, sailors from the Kronstadt naval base, and troops of the Petrograd garrison occupied the telegraph and telephone exchanges, the state bank, railway stations, and bridges across the Neva with almost no resistance. Kerensky slipped out of the city in a car flying an American flag to seek loyal troops. At 21:40 on the evening of 7 November the cruiser Aurora, moored on the Neva, fired a blank round as a signal, and after several hours of confused negotiation with the palace defenders, a mixed force of Cossacks, cadets, and the Women's Battalion, Red Guards entered the Winter Palace through unguarded doors and, at about two in the morning on 8 November, arrested the ministers of the Provisional Government in a corner room on the second floor.

While the palace was being taken, the Congress of Soviets met at the Smolny Institute. The Menshevik and Right Socialist Revolutionary delegates walked out in protest. The remaining majority, mostly Bolsheviks and Left Socialist Revolutionaries, ratified the transfer of power and passed the first two Soviet decrees, drafted by Lenin: a decree on peace calling for an immediate armistice without annexations or indemnities, and a decree on land that abolished private landlord property and handed it to the peasant soviets. A new government, styled the Council of People's Commissars (Sovnarkom) and chaired by Lenin, was formed the same day.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: The Winter Palace on 26 October 1917, the day after the Bolsheviks stormed it and arrested the Provisional Government.

sections.body:
The Bolshevik grip was narrow at first: a party of some 340,000 members in a country of 150 million, with authority barely reaching outside the industrial cities. Elections to a Constituent Assembly, long promised, were held on schedule in late November 1917 and returned a large majority for the peasant-based Socialist Revolutionaries. The assembly met for a single day, 18 January 1918, before being dispersed by Red Guards at Lenin's order.

On 3 March 1918 the Soviet government signed the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk with Germany and its allies, surrendering Poland, Ukraine, the Baltic provinces, and Finland to end Russian participation in the war. The concession was catastrophic on paper but freed the regime to fight the civil war that broke out in the summer, in which White armies under Denikin, Kolchak, Yudenich, and Wrangel, supported by British, French, American, Japanese, and other intervening forces, tried to strangle the revolution. The Red Army, built from scratch by Trotsky, held Moscow, to which the capital was moved in March 1918, and by 1920 had defeated the Whites and reconquered Ukraine, the Caucasus, and much of Central Asia. The Romanov family, held under guard at Yekaterinburg, was murdered on the night of 16-17 July 1918.

The civil war and the accompanying 'war communism' cost several million lives to combat, famine, and typhus. In 1921 Lenin's regime retreated to a mixed economy under the New Economic Policy; in December 1922 the reconquered territories were reorganised as the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics. Lenin, disabled by strokes from 1922, died in January 1924. The struggle for succession, won by Joseph Stalin against Trotsky and other rivals, would take the Soviet state into forced collectivisation, industrial five-year plans, and the terror of the 1930s.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Bolshevik seizure of power was the first successful revolution to install a self-consciously Marxist government and to claim the mantle of international socialist revolution. It inspired short-lived risings in Germany, Hungary, and Bavaria in 1918-19, the founding of the Communist International in Moscow in 1919, and a global network of parties that took orders and doctrine from the Soviet capital. For much of the twentieth century the Soviet Union was the alternative pole to liberal capitalism, and communist parties in China, Vietnam, Cuba, and elsewhere would trace their genealogy to October 1917.

At home the revolution transformed almost every social relation. Land was redistributed to peasants and then, brutally, taken back into collective farms; women received equal legal rights, divorce was liberalised, and by the 1930s female employment reached levels unseen in the West; literacy campaigns brought basic reading and writing to tens of millions of adults. The costs were also without precedent in Russian history: mass political violence, famines that killed millions in Ukraine and Kazakhstan in the early 1930s, and a security state that penetrated every institution.

The Soviet Union survived the German invasion of 1941, defeated the Wehrmacht at Stalingrad and Kursk, and emerged from the Second World War as one of two superpowers, with its writ extending across eastern Europe and into East Asia. It also produced the counter-model that shaped Western politics: the Cold War, the arms race, and the ideological competition that ended only with the Soviet collapse in December 1991. Historians still argue whether October 1917 was a genuine popular revolution or a party coup; there is little argument about its scale as a hinge in modern history.

worldContext.text: The United States had entered the First World War in April 1917 and by November was ramping up conscription, war production, and the first shipments of the American Expeditionary Force to France under General John Pershing. The Espionage Act of June and the coming Sedition Act muzzled dissent; racial tensions produced deadly riots at East St Louis in July. In Mexico the constitution promulgated at Queretaro in February 1917 laid the foundations of the post-revolutionary state, though fighting between Carranza, Villa, and Zapata continued in the countryside.

The Middle East in November 1917 was being remade by war and diplomacy. British forces under General Edmund Allenby took Beersheba on 31 October and would enter Jerusalem on 9 December, ending four centuries of Ottoman rule. On 2 November British Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour signed the letter to Lord Rothschild that promised a 'national home for the Jewish people' in Palestine. The Arab Revolt led by Sharif Hussein of Mecca continued along the Hejaz railway with the assistance of British officers including T. E. Lawrence.

China in 1917 was fragmenting into rival warlord fiefdoms after the death of Yuan Shikai. In July the monarchist Zhang Xun briefly restored the boy-emperor Puyi in Beijing before being driven out. In September Sun Yat-sen set up a rival government in Guangzhou. On 14 August China declared war on Germany and Austria-Hungary, largely to secure a seat at the coming peace conference. Japan, its Taisho democracy still functioning, moved to consolidate wartime gains in Shandong and the Pacific.

British India in 1917 was straining under the war effort. On 20 August the Secretary of State for India, Edwin Montagu, announced in the Commons the goal of the 'progressive realisation of responsible government' in India, a formulation that would produce the Montagu-Chelmsford reforms of 1919. Gandhi's first mass campaign on Indian soil, on behalf of indigo-growing peasants at Champaran in Bihar, was under way. In April 1917 the British had renamed the Mesopotamian city of Baghdad the seat of a new occupation administration.

The German commander Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck was still tying down large Allied forces in East Africa with a mobile guerrilla campaign; in late November 1917 he crossed the Rovuma River into Portuguese Mozambique. In South Africa, wartime demand accelerated industrial growth and African urban migration. The tenant-farming and dispossession of Africans under the 1913 Natives Land Act was hardening, and in West Africa French recruitment of tens of thousands of tirailleurs senegalais for the trenches produced resistance from the Volta to the Niger.

Australia and New Zealand were in the final and grimmest year of their Western Front commitment. Australian divisions had fought through Bullecourt, Messines, and the Third Battle of Ypres, better known as Passchendaele, which dragged into the mud of November 1917. Prime Minister Billy Hughes had lost a bitterly divisive referendum on conscription for overseas service in October 1917, the second such defeat, leaving Australia's expeditionary force dependent on volunteers to the end of the war.

figures.name: Vladimir Ilich Lenin
figures.role: Bolshevik leader, chairman of the new Council of People's Commissars

figures.name: Leon Trotsky
figures.role: Chairman of the Petrograd Soviet's Military Revolutionary Committee and later founder of the Red Army

figures.name: Alexander Kerensky
figures.role: Prime minister of the Provisional Government, overthrown on the night of 7-8 November 1917

figures.name: Joseph Stalin
figures.role: Georgian Bolshevik and member of the Central Committee, later general secretary of the party
figures.credit: US Library of Congress, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Nicholas II
figures.role: Last Romanov tsar, abdicated in March 1917 and later murdered with his family at Yekaterinburg in July 1918
figures.credit: Boasson and Eggler, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The Winter Palace in Petrograd on 26 October 1917, the day after the Bolshevik-led Military Revolutionary Committee stormed it and arrested the Provisional Government.
images.credit: Pyotr Novitsky (1885 to 1942), via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The cruiser Aurora, whose blank round on the evening of 7 November 1917 signalled the assault on the Winter Palace.
Provisional Government troops firing on demonstrators in Petrograd during the July Days of 1917, an episode that led Lenin briefly into hiding.
